dialogue.utterance: Unit 6 respond for a medical alarm at 1234 Maple Street.
Hi sir, I'm Taylor, an EMT with the rescue squad. What was the reason for calling 911 today?
I've been short of breath all morning and just got worse. I hit my medical alarm.
You appear awake. Can you tell me your full name?
James Smith.
And if I ask a simple question, can you answer coherently?
If I ask you to raise your left hand, can you do that for me?
Yes, I can.
When did you first notice you were short of breath?
On a scale of 1 to 10, how severe is your shortness of breath right now?
How would you describe the feeling-tight, like you can't take a full breath?
Does it go to your chest, back, or arms at all?
Are you experiencing wheezing or any other breathing noises?
Do you have any medical history I should know about?
I have COPD and congestive heart failure.
What medications do you normally take?
I take Lasix, aspirin, atorvastatin, Coreg, Proscar, and Cozaar.
Do you have any drug allergies?
No known drug allergies.
What have you been doing today before realizing you were short of breath?
Your airway is clear and patent.
You're breathing rapidly at a rate of about 39 per minute and shallow.
I hear wheezing and rhonchi in both lungs. Your breathing sounds are consistent with your COPD history.
Your radial pulse is strong and regular. You appear to be perfusing well.
Pulse is 135. That's elevated.
Breathing rate is 39-very rapid.
Blood pressure is 218 over 121. Quite high.
Oxygen saturation is 80%. That's very low.
Glucose is 138. That's within normal range.
EKG is showing sinus tachycardia.
Mr. Smith, we are going to apply CPAP now to help your breathing and increase oxygen.
CPAP is now on. Just breathe normally through the mask.
We are giving you a breathing treatment with Albuterol and Ipratropium through the CPAP.
It should help open up your airways and improve your breathing.
Your skin is warm, dry, and normal in color.
Eyes are open and reactive to light. Pupils are equal and round.
No airway obstruction or issues with your throat or nasal passages.
I'll listen again. Your wheeze is still present, but it seems less intense.
We're doing a 4 lead EKG. Just relax, no pain expected.
The 12 lead is showing sinus tachycardia, some peaked T waves, left axis deviation, and possible signs of left ventricular hypertrophy.
We administered 10mg of Dexamethasone intramuscularly to help reduce airway inflammation.
We attempted IV access twice in your arms and legs but were unsuccessful.
Pulse is now 137, and breathing is slightly down to 32.
Blood pressure is now 202 over 117.
Oxygen saturation has improved to 95%. That's a positive change.
Your glucose is not registering properly right now. Your blood sugar will be rechecked at the hospital.
Let me check your extremities for any deformities or pulses.
Peripheral pulses are good and no visible deformities.
No new changes in the 4 lead EKG, still showing sinus tachycardia.
Pressing gently on your belly-no pain or tenderness reported.
Since the beginning, you've reported only difficulty with breathing, not with pain.
When was the last time you ate or drank anything?
Probably after breakfast this morning.
Are you taking all of your medications regularly?
Yes, I've been following my routine. My symptoms began to steadily worsen this morning.
Any problems with urination or frequency?
No, just the breathing.
We're going to transport you emergently to [COMPANY_NAME] for worsened hypoxia and COPD exacerbation.
You're now in the stairchair and being transferred to the stretcher with a secure 5 point harness.

Male, 78, takes Lasix, aspirin, atorvastatin, Coreg, Proscar, and Cozaar. No drug allergies. Alert, oriented, with shortness of breath likely due to COPD. Vitals: BP 218/121, P 135, R 39, SpO2 80 initially, now 95 with CPAP. CPAP administered, DuoNeb (Albuterol and Ipratropium), IM Dexamethasone, and 12 lead EKG performed showing possible lateral issues and left ventricular hypertrophy.
Two IV attempts failed and patient remains stable. Medicals and vitals well documented.
Breathing rate is 32, which is still high but improved.
CPAP continues to function as expected and oxygen is maintaining well.
You're safely secured for transport.
We are ready for transport and en route to [COMPANY_NAME]. Vitals will be continuously monitored.
Battle checks are clear. All equipment set for transfer to the ambulance.
You are ready for the ride. I will monitor you all the way to the hospital.
No significant worsening detected in lung sounds.
Boarding complete, ready to proceed to the emergency department.
Pre-hospital EKG and vital data will be summarized upon arrival for the ER.
All transportation systems verified safe to begin movement.
Please let me know if you feel any sudden discomfort while we head to the hospital.
[COMPANY_NAME] is aware and prepared for Mr. Smith's arrival.
We are rolling. You will reach the hospital in approximately 5-10 minutes.
Patient is on CPAP, stable with improved baseline oxygen and stable reviewed vitals.
All systems up and running for a safe ferrying.
Ambulance is moving. Continue to breathe but stay still so the machines can monitor your progress.
Our final assessment is ready. No further pertinent information at this time.
The wheels are rolling, and we're go for transport. Awaiting hospital arrival.
Please stay seated and be secure for the rest of the drive.
We're moving smoothly with a medium level of traffic.
Everything is ready for handoff following hospital arrival.
Your condition is remaining stable, let's arrive safely to the hospital.
Patient safe for Edwin with current status.
